title: AI push not protectionism: Charlton
full_text: Other Editions 01 May 2026 TheNation AI push not protectionism: Charlton The Australian Matthew Cranston. Economics Correspondent, Exclusive 1 May 2026 672 words English AUSTLN 4 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. The Albanese government will demand the private sector dramatically lift its rate of investment in Australian AI or risk a near-term hollowing out of industry and an exodus of data overseas. AI Assistant Minister Andrew Charlton will insist in a speech on Friday to Australia’s biggest companies including banks NAB, Macquarie, CBA, Westpac, Telstra and Optus that they should lead the charge in buying and investing in local AI. “Some will say this is all protectionism dressed up as patriotism. It is not,” Dr Charlton will say. “I am not asking anyone to buy an inferior product. I’m asking Australian businesses: when you have a choice between a foreign AI product and an Australian one, and the choice is close – and often it is close – lean Australian.” Dr Charlton, who will give the address at the University of Technology Sydney in a sold-out event, is warning that jobs will be lost and data will vanish overseas if business does not reconsider its reliance on overseas AI. “When you outsource that capability to a foreign vendor, you don’t just buy a product, you create a path of dependency. You hollow out local expertise. You hand over globally unique data with no value captured for the nation. “Australia is standing at its own sliding doors moment on artificial intelligence.” The federal government has already changed its own procurement rules for AI, including forcing government agencies to approach Australian businesses first, before all others, for procurements that fall below Free Trade Agreement thresholds. There is also an explicit requirement that economic benefit to the Australian economy is considered as part of the value for money assessment for all government procurements valued at $1m or more. Dr Charlton will not take aim at the AI giants such as Microsoft’s Co-pilot, ChatGPT, Google’s Gemini, and Anthropic’s Claude but will instead start a campaign for Australian companies. He will call out how I-MED Radiology Network – one of the world’s largest private radiology companies based in Sydney – took a chance on Harrison.ai, an Australian company founded in 2018 and now used in healthcare facilities across more than 40 countries, including powering every public emergency department CAT scan in Hong Kong. The company has raised more than $300m, and has been backed with a $32m equity investment from the National Reconstruction Fund. “Government policy settings alone will not get us there,” Dr Charlton will say. “Government can set the conditions, send signals, and de-risk early-stage companies. What it cannot do is replace the procurement decisions of thousands of private-­sector organisations. That is where the real leverage lies.” Dr Charlton also notes that some companies don’t always take the Australian option, with BreastScreen NSW choosing a Korean company instead. “They had to make an important and consequential procurement decision about which technology they would go with. In one corner was an Australian option – the BreastCancer Screening with Artificial Intelligence program led by Associate Professor Helen Frazer. But BreastScreen NSW didn’t choose BRAIx or any other Australian capability. They went with Lunit – a Korean-listed company whose algorithm was trained primarily on women from Korea, UK and the USA, with no peer-reviewed results in an Australian setting.” Dr Charlton will also press home the importance of adopting Australian AI for the purpose of appeasing unions and workers. Unions have already given the green light to the Albanese government to reject the need for a stand-alone artificial intelligence act, and take a light touch on regulating the technology. “Governments understand Australian workers and unions will also benefit from more sovereign AI. If the systems transforming Australian workplaces are all built offshore, workers will have no seat at the table when those systems are designed. “Unions, employers and government must work together to uplift skills and translate higher productivity into a higher standard of living for all Australians.” The Australian-20260501-Australian-4-000500569927 Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: The Albanese government is urging the private sector to increase investment in Australian artificial intelligence (AI) to prevent job losses and data exodus overseas. AI Assistant Minister Andrew Charlton emphasised the importance of choosing local AI solutions over foreign options when they are comparable. The government has also adjusted procurement rules to prioritise Australian businesses in AI-related contracts.

heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: The government is pushing for increased private sector investment in local AI to avoid dependency on foreign products.
Charlton highlighted the risk of job losses and data leaving Australia if local AI options are not chosen.
New procurement rules require government agencies to consider Australian businesses first for AI contracts below certain thresholds.
relevance: This initiative impacts the financial services sector by influencing procurement decisions and investment strategies in technology.
